narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 59.8% chance that you would not pass—above the threshold we use for admissions decisions. This prediction draws from patterns in data from thousands of past students, focusing on academic and background factors like grades, test scores, and family circumstances, while setting aside demographics like gender or race. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 grade decile, which placed you in the bottom tier at 1.00, far below the average of around 5.91 for students who went on to pass the bar. Next in importance was your family income in the lower-middle bracket, which actually worked in your favor by pulling the prediction slightly toward passing, as this background is more common among successful bar takers than lower incomes, though less frequent than middle or upper-middle ones. Your LSAT score of 24 also contributed toward the failure prediction, standing well below the typical 37.27 average for those who passed. Other elements, like your undergraduate GPA of 3.00 (a bit under the 3.24 average for passers) and full-time study plans (common among successful students), played smaller roles. Compared to admitted students, who generally show stronger Year 3 performance and higher LSATs, your profile aligned more closely with those predicted to struggle on the bar. This isn't a judgment on your potential or effort—many with similar starts have succeeded by addressing key gaps. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your later-year grades through targeted study or additional coursework, and exploring programs with bar passage support tailored to diverse backgrounds. You might also seek mentorship from Hispanic alumni networks or women in law groups for personalized strategies. Reapplying after these improvements could shift your predicted odds significantly, opening doors to approval. Keep pushing; your determination as a full-time aspiring lawyer from a lower-middle background positions you well for a comeback story.
